story:
Four years out of the Air Force, unemployed, I turned the gym into my mission. Monday through Friday I clanged and banged iron; weekends I hit the treadmill. Even at home I rehearsed sets and techniques. The work showed, and people noticed. One pair of eyes stood out—Avery, a young regular in a ball cap whose gaze lingered in the mirror.

After weeks of easy smiles and near-misses by the water fountain, she started drifting into my space more often. I kept my rule: the gym is my sanctuary; I don’t mix lifting with flirting. Still, a simple, harmless opener felt fine. We both wore Air Jordans. One day beside the lat pulldown, I pulled my headphones and said, “Nice shoes.” She barely looked up. Short, cool answers—UT sophomore, marketing—no questions back, no warmth. I cut it off and went back to work.

Next day she avoided me entirely. A nagging tennis elbow and the awkwardness pushed me to take a few days off. When I returned, the manager stopped me at the desk. A member had reported feeling harassed. Zero tolerance. Membership revoked. No details. I’d been lifting there for three years and talked to almost no one. As I stood there, Avery walked past without a glance. After the manager left, the front desk attendant quietly told me it was her—and that she’d reported someone else before. The gym didn’t want drama or risk. I walked out stunned. I’d asked about her shoes and, when it didn’t land, I backed off. I wasn’t chasing anything serious; I just wanted a friendly hello between sets.

I took a few weeks to reset, then joined another gym. First day—chest day—head down, headphones on. A brunette approached and asked if I used to go to the EOS off Dale Mabry. She’d seen me there, said she liked that I focused on my workout instead of prowling like some of the guys. She’d come to this gym for the same reason: older crowd, more respectful, no cloud of weed in the locker rooms. Her name was Angie.

We traded quick impressions of the place. Then she asked if I needed a spot on my incline bench. I added a little weight, told her five reps, and pushed them up with her steady hands ready. She said I had it, then headed back to her routine. Later, after I finished with dumbbells, she walked over, asked for my phone, tapped in her number, and said, “Text me. We’ll get a protein shake sometime.” The text came through right away: “Great meeting you. Looking good.”

Getting banned still stung, but it clarified what I already believed: keep it respectful, keep it simple, and if it’s not welcome, move on. I wasn’t trying to turn the gym into a dating app. I’m there to train, nod to decent people, and leave stronger than I came. Angie seemed to understand that. As for the shake—chocolate or vanilla—either one works. And no need to talk shoes.
